annotations.label: First-order logic

anchor.snippet: is a type of formal system used in mathematics

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes first-order logic via FirstOrder.Language together with Structure, Term, BoundedFormula, and Realize.

annotations.label: Theory

anchor.snippet: is usually a first-order logic together with a specified domain of discourse

annotations.note: A theory is defined as a set of sentences (Set L.Sentence).

annotations.label: First-order vs higher-order logic

anchor.snippet: distinguishes first-order logic from higher-order logic

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory only formalizes first-order quantification over elements; higher-order logic and the distinction are absent.

annotations.label: Predicate

anchor.snippet: A predicate evaluates to true or false for an entity or entities in the domain of discourse

annotations.note: Predicates correspond to interpreted relation symbols via Structure.RelMap (relation → Prop), but "predicate" is not a standalone definition.

annotations.label: Interpretation (model)

anchor.snippet: An interpretation (or model) of a first-order formula specifies what each predicate means

annotations.note: An interpretation is a FirstOrder.Language.Structure assigning meanings (funMap, RelMap) to all symbols.

annotations.label: Terms and formulas

anchor.snippet: There are two key types of well-formed expressions: terms

annotations.note: Terms and formulas are the inductive types FirstOrder.Language.Term and FirstOrder.Language.BoundedFormula.

annotations.label: Logical and non-logical symbols

anchor.snippet: It is common to divide the symbols of the alphabet into logical symbols

annotations.note: A Language carries only non-logical symbols (Functions/Relations); logical symbols are built into BoundedFormula's constructors, but the explicit division is not a named concept.

annotations.label: Sufficiency of symbols

anchor.snippet: Either one of the quantifiers along with negation

annotations.note: Mathlib derives ∃, ⊓, ⊔, ⊤ from ⊥, imp, and all (e.g. BoundedFormula.ex := φ.not.all.not), but expressive completeness is not proved as a theorem.

annotations.label: Signature

anchor.snippet: This choice is made via a signature

annotations.note: A signature is a FirstOrder.Language, given by Functions and Relations indexed by arity.

annotations.label: Predicate symbol

anchor.snippet: A predicate symbol (or relation symbol ) with some valence

annotations.note: Relation symbols of valence n are the type Language.Relations n.

annotations.label: Function symbol

anchor.snippet: A function symbol , with some valence greater than or equal to 0

annotations.note: Function symbols of arity n are the type Language.Functions n.

annotations.label: Constant symbol

anchor.snippet: Function symbols of valence 0 are called constant symbols

annotations.note: Constants are defined as Language.Constants := L.Functions 0, the arity-0 function symbols.

annotations.label: Formation rules

anchor.snippet: The formation rules define the terms and formulas of first-order logic

annotations.note: The formation rules are the inductive definitions of Term and BoundedFormula.

annotations.label: Terms

anchor.snippet: The set of terms is inductively defined by the following rules

annotations.note: Term is inductively defined with var and func constructors exactly matching the formation rules.

annotations.label: Formulas (well-formed formulas)

anchor.snippet: The set of formulas (also called well-formed formulas

annotations.note: Well-formed formulas are the inductive BoundedFormula (and Formula := BoundedFormula α 0).

annotations.label: Atomic formulas

anchor.snippet: The formulas obtained from the first rule are said to be atomic formulas

annotations.note: IsAtomic characterizes formulas that are equalities or relations applied to terms.

annotations.label: Unique readability of formulas

anchor.snippet: This property is known as unique readability of formulas

annotations.note: Formulas are an inductive datatype, so unique readability holds automatically via constructor injectivity, but it is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Operator precedence conventions

anchor.snippet: conventions have been developed about the precedence of the logical operators

annotations.note: Operator precedence is a parsing/notation convention, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Free and bound variables

anchor.snippet: In a formula, a variable may occur free or bound

annotations.note: BoundedFormula structurally separates free variables (indexed by α, computed by freeVarFinset) from bound variables (de Bruijn indices in Fin n).

annotations.label: First-order sentence

anchor.snippet: A formula in first-order logic with no free variable occurrences is called a first-order sentence

annotations.note: Sentence := Formula Empty, a formula with no free variables.

annotations.label: Language of ordered abelian groups

anchor.snippet: In mathematics, the language of ordered abelian groups has one constant symbol

annotations.note: Mathlib has the order language (single ≤ relation) and a separate ring language, but not the combined ordered-abelian-group signature (0, −, +, ≤).

annotations.label: Interpretation of a first-order language

anchor.snippet: An interpretation of a first-order language assigns a denotation to each non-logical symbol

annotations.note: A Structure assigns denotations to non-logical symbols via funMap and RelMap.

annotations.label: Structure (model)

anchor.snippet: The most common way of specifying an interpretation

annotations.note: FirstOrder.Language.Structure is the class of first-order structures on a carrier type.

annotations.label: Domain of discourse

anchor.snippet: The domain of discourse D is a nonempty set of

annotations.note: The domain is just the carrier type M (taken Nonempty in models), not a named definition.

annotations.label: Interpretation of non-logical symbols

anchor.snippet: Non-logical symbols are interpreted as follows

annotations.note: Structure.funMap and Structure.RelMap interpret function and relation symbols respectively.

annotations.label: Variable assignment

anchor.snippet: A formula evaluates to true or false given an interpretation and a variable assignment

annotations.note: Variable assignments are represented as functions α → M (and Fin n → M) passed to Term.realize and BoundedFormula.Realize, not a named structure.

annotations.label: T-schema

anchor.snippet: The inductive definition used to make this assignment is called the T-schema

annotations.note: BoundedFormula.Realize is the inductive Tarskian truth definition over the formula constructors.

annotations.label: Alternate truth definition

anchor.snippet: There is a second common approach to defining truth values

annotations.note: Mathlib defines truth only via the Tarski recursion (Realize); the alternative satisfaction-relation approach is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalence of truth definitions

anchor.snippet: This alternate approach gives exactly the same truth values to all sentences

annotations.note: Only one truth definition (Realize) exists in Mathlib, so this equivalence between two definitions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Satisfiability

anchor.snippet: A sentence is satisfiable if there is some interpretation under which it is true

annotations.note: Theory.IsSatisfiable means a theory has a nonempty model; sentence satisfiability is the singleton case.

annotations.label: Satisfiability with free variables

anchor.snippet: Satisfiability of formulas with free variables is more complicated

annotations.note: Open formulas are evaluated by Formula.Realize with a variable assignment, but a dedicated satisfiability notion for free-variable formulas is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Logically valid formula

anchor.snippet: A formula is logically valid (or simply valid ) if it is true in every interpretation

annotations.note: Validity is the empty-theory case (∅ ⊨ᵇ φ) of Theory.ModelsBoundedFormula, true in all interpretations.

annotations.label: Logical consequence

anchor.snippet: is a logical consequence of a formula

annotations.note: T ⊨ᵇ φ (ModelsBoundedFormula) means φ holds in every model of T, i.e. semantic logical consequence.

annotations.label: Tarski–Givant relation algebra equivalence

anchor.snippet: showed that the fragment of first-order logic that has no atomic sentence lying in the scope of more than three quantifiers has the same expressive power as relation algebra

annotations.note: Relation algebra and this expressive-power equivalence are not present in Mathlib's model theory.

annotations.label: First-order theory

anchor.section: First-order theories, models, and elementary classes
anchor.snippet: A first-order theory of a particular signature is a set of axioms

annotations.note: A theory is Set L.Sentence, a set of sentences (axioms) over a signature.

annotations.label: Model and elementary class

anchor.section: First-order theories, models, and elementary classes
anchor.snippet: A first-order structure that satisfies all sentences in a given theory is said to be a model of the theory

annotations.note: Theory.Model (M ⊨ T) defines a model satisfying every sentence; the notion of "elementary class" itself is not separately named.

annotations.label: Existence of nonstandard models

anchor.section: First-order theories, models, and elementary classes
anchor.snippet: the Löwenheim–Skolem theorem shows that most first-order theories will also have other, nonstandard models

annotations.note: exists_model_card_eq yields models of other cardinalities for theories with an infinite model, but "nonstandard model" is not a named concept.

annotations.label: Consistent and complete theory

anchor.section: First-order theories, models, and elementary classes
anchor.snippet: A theory is consistent (within a deductive system )

annotations.note: Mathlib has the semantic IsComplete and IsSatisfiable (consistency), but not the deductive-system-relative (syntactic) notions described.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorem

anchor.section: First-order theories, models, and elementary classes
anchor.snippet: Gödel's incompleteness theorem shows that effective first-order theories that include a sufficient portion of the arithmetic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no deductive system or arithmetic-encoding machinery, so Gödel's incompleteness theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Empty domains and rules of inference

anchor.snippet: Many common rules of inference are valid only when the domain of discourse is required to be nonempty

annotations.note: Mathlib's models/satisfiability assume Nonempty carriers, reflecting the nonempty-domain requirement, but rules of inference themselves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Deductive system

anchor.snippet: A deductive system is used to demonstrate, on a purely syntactic basis

annotations.note: Mathlib's model theory is purely semantic; no syntactic deductive system is formalized.

annotations.label: Sound and complete deductive system

anchor.snippet: A deductive system is sound if any formula that can be derived in the system is logically valid

annotations.note: No deductive system exists in Mathlib, so soundness/completeness of derivability are not formalized.

annotations.label: Semidecidability of logical consequence

anchor.snippet: logical consequence in first-order logic is only semidecidable

annotations.note: No decidability/computability analysis of first-order consequence is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule of inference

anchor.snippet: A rule of inference states that, given a particular formula

annotations.note: Inference rules belong to a deductive system, which Mathlib does not formalize.

annotations.label: Substitution rule

anchor.snippet: The substitution rule states that for any φ and any term t

annotations.note: Substitution of terms for variables is formalized (BoundedFormula.subst), but not packaged as a derivation/inference rule.

annotations.label: Hilbert-style deductive system

anchor.snippet: A deduction in a Hilbert-style deductive system is a list of formulas

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style proof calculus is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural deduction systems

anchor.snippet: Natural deduction systems resemble Hilbert-style systems

annotations.note: Natural deduction for first-order logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sequent

anchor.snippet: it uses sequents , which are expressions of the form

annotations.note: Sequents and sequent calculus are not formalized in Mathlib's model theory.

annotations.label: Tableaux method

anchor.snippet: the derivations in the tableaux method are not lists of formulas

annotations.note: The tableaux proof method is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Resolution rule sound and complete

anchor.snippet: The resolution rule is a single rule of inference that, together with unification

annotations.note: Resolution and unification are not formalized in Mathlib's model theory.

annotations.label: Provable identities

anchor.snippet: Many identities can be proved, which establish equivalences between particular formulas

annotations.note: Semantic equivalence of formulas (φ ⇔[T] ψ) and many realize-equivalence lemmas are formalized, giving these identities semantically rather than via provability.

annotations.label: First-order logic with equality

anchor.snippet: The most common convention, known as first-order logic with equality

annotations.note: Equality is a primitive logical symbol (BoundedFormula.equal / Term.bdEqual) interpreted as genuine equality in Realize.

annotations.label: Reflexivity axiom

anchor.snippet: For each variable x , x = x

annotations.note: Since equality is realized as honest =, x = x holds automatically (Eq.refl) and is not stated as a separate first-order equality axiom.

annotations.label: Substitution for functions axiom

anchor.snippet: For all variables x and y , and any function symbol f

annotations.note: Function symbols are interpreted by genuine functions (funMap), so congruence under equality holds automatically (congrArg) rather than as a stated axiom.

annotations.label: Substitution for formulas (Leibniz's law)

anchor.snippet: For any variables x and y and any formula

annotations.note: Because equality is genuine =, substitution of equals in formulas holds via congruence of Realize rather than as a first-order equality axiom.

annotations.label: Symmetry and transitivity of equality

anchor.snippet: Many other properties of equality are consequences of the axioms above

annotations.note: Equality is genuine =, so symmetry/transitivity hold via Eq.symm/Eq.trans rather than being derived from first-order equality axioms.

annotations.label: First-order logic without equality

anchor.snippet: This convention is known as first-order logic without equality

annotations.note: Mathlib's BoundedFormula always includes the equality constructor; an equality-free variant is not formalized.

annotations.label: Normal model

anchor.snippet: the term normal model is used to refer to an interpretation where no distinct individuals

annotations.note: Equality is always interpreted as genuine equality in Mathlib, so the normal/non-normal model distinction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Theory with equality

anchor.snippet: the theory is said to have equality, or to be a theory with equality

annotations.note: Defining a congruence relation that behaves like equality within a theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ad hoc definitions of equality

anchor.snippet: Some theories allow other ad hoc definitions of equality

annotations.note: Ad hoc internal definitions of equality are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel's completeness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel's completeness theorem , proved by Kurt Gödel in 1929, establishes that there are sound, complete, effective deductive systems

annotations.note: Mathlib has no deductive system, so the completeness theorem relating provability to semantic validity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Semidecidability of first-order consequence

anchor.snippet: Thus first-order logical consequence is semidecidable

annotations.note: Semidecidability of first-order consequence is a computability result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Undecidability of first-order logic

anchor.snippet: first-order logic is undecidable (although semidecidable)

annotations.note: Church–Turing undecidability of first-order validity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decidable fragments exist

anchor.snippet: There are systems weaker than full first-order logic for which the logical consequence relation is decidable

annotations.note: Decidability of fragments of first-order logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-variable counting fragment C2

anchor.snippet: FOL with two variables and the counting quantifiers

annotations.note: The two-variable counting fragment C2 and its decidability are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monadic first-order fragment

anchor.snippet: monadic first-order fragment (MFO, or Löwenheim fragment)

annotations.note: The monadic (Löwenheim) fragment and its decidability are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Löb–Gurevich fragment

anchor.snippet: Löb–Gurevich fragment: FOL without equality, with only unary function symbols

annotations.note: The Löb–Gurevich fragment is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rabin fragment

anchor.snippet: Rabin fragment: FOL with equality, with exactly one unary function symbol

annotations.note: The Rabin fragment is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bernays–Schönfinkel–Ramsey fragment

anchor.snippet: Bernays–Schönfinkel–Ramsey fragment: all relational first-order sentences in prenex normal form

annotations.note: Although prenex normal form (IsPrenex) is formalized, the BSR fragment and its decidability are not.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorem

anchor.snippet: The Löwenheim–Skolem theorem shows that if a first-order theory of cardinality

annotations.note: The Downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem is formalized (with the upward direction as exists_elementaryEmbedding_card_eq in Satisfiability).

annotations.label: Infinite structures not categorically axiomatizable

anchor.snippet: The Löwenheim–Skolem theorem implies that infinite structures cannot be categorically axiomatized in first-order logic

annotations.note: exists_model_card_eq gives models of every sufficiently large cardinality, implying no infinite structure is categorically axiomatizable, though not stated in that form.

annotations.label: Compactness theorem

anchor.snippet: The compactness theorem states that a set of first-order sentences has a model if and only if every finite subset of it has a model

annotations.note: This is exactly the compactness theorem: a theory is satisfiable iff finitely satisfiable.

annotations.label: Finite graphs not an elementary class

anchor.snippet: the class of all finite graphs is not an elementary class

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the theory of simple graphs but not the non-axiomatizability of the class of finite graphs.

annotations.label: Connected graphs not an elementary class

anchor.snippet: connected graphs are not an elementary class in first-order logic

annotations.note: Non-axiomatizability of connectedness in first-order logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lindström's theorem

anchor.snippet: Per Lindström showed that the metalogical properties just discussed actually characterize first-order logic

annotations.note: Lindström's characterization theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No categorical first-order theory with infinite model

anchor.snippet: if a first-order theory has any infinite model, then it has infinite models of every cardinality

annotations.note: exists_model_card_eq shows a theory with an infinite model has a model of every sufficiently large cardinality.

annotations.label: Formalizing natural language

anchor.snippet: First-order logic is able to formalize many simple quantifier constructions in natural language

annotations.note: Claims about formalizing natural-language quantifiers are informal and not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reducing logical symbols

anchor.snippet: First-order logic can be studied in languages with fewer logical symbols than were described above

annotations.note: BoundedFormula uses a minimal set of primitives (falsum, imp, all, equal, rel) and derives the others, realizing this reduction in its construction.

annotations.label: Eliminating function and constant symbols

anchor.snippet: It is possible to entirely avoid function symbols and constant symbols

annotations.note: Relational and algebraic languages (IsRelational) exist, but the function-to-relation elimination reduction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Restricting arities via pairing function

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to restrict the arities of function symbols and predicate symbols

annotations.note: Reducing arities via a pairing function is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Many-sorted first-order logic

anchor.snippet: Many-sorted first-order logic allows variables to have different sorts

annotations.note: Mathlib's first-order logic is single-sorted; many-sorted logic is not formalized.

annotations.label: Reduction to single-sorted logic

anchor.snippet: many-sorted first-order logic can be reduced to single-sorted first-order logic

annotations.note: The reduction of many-sorted to single-sorted logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniqueness quantification

anchor.snippet: This notation, called uniqueness quantification

annotations.note: ExistsUnique (∃!) is defined as ∃ x, p x ∧ ∀ y, p y → y = x at the ambient logic level, matching uniqueness quantification (not a BoundedFormula constructor).

annotations.label: Extra quantifiers

anchor.snippet: First-order logic with extra quantifiers has new quantifiers

annotations.note: Generalized quantifiers (e.g. "there exist infinitely many") are not added to Mathlib's BoundedFormula.

annotations.label: Infinitary logic

anchor.snippet: Infinitary logic allows infinitely long sentences

annotations.note: Mathlib's formulas are finite; infinitary logic is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinitary logic notation L_αβ

anchor.snippet: The most commonly studied infinitary logics are denoted

annotations.note: The L_αβ infinitary logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intuitionistic first-order logic

anchor.snippet: Intuitionistic first-order logic uses intuitionistic rather than classical reasoning

annotations.note: Mathlib's model theory uses classical semantics; intuitionistic first-order logic is not formalized as an object.

annotations.label: First-order modal logic

anchor.snippet: First-order modal logic allows one to describe other possible worlds

annotations.note: First-order modal logic and possible-worlds semantics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order fuzzy logics

anchor.snippet: First-order fuzzy logics are first-order extensions of propositional fuzzy logics

annotations.note: First-order fuzzy logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fixpoint logic

anchor.snippet: Fixpoint logic extends first-order logic by adding the closure under the least fixed points

annotations.note: Fixpoint logic is not formalized in Mathlib's model theory.

annotations.label: Second-order logic

anchor.snippet: Second-order logic extends first-order logic by adding the latter type of quantification

annotations.note: Mathlib's Language supports only first-order quantification over elements; second-order logic is not formalized.

annotations.label: Second-order consequence not semidecidable

anchor.snippet: the (semantic) logical consequence relation for second-order and higher-order logic is not semidecidable

annotations.note: Decidability properties of second-order consequence are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metalogical theorems fail in higher-order logic

anchor.snippet: the Löwenheim–Skolem theorem and compactness theorem of first-order logic become false when generalized

annotations.note: Failure of Löwenheim–Skolem and compactness in higher-order logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Automated theorem proving

anchor.section: Automated theorem proving and formal methods
anchor.snippet: Automated theorem proving refers to the development of computer programs that search and find derivations

annotations.note: Automated theorem proving is a description of software, not a mathematical statement formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Model checking for first-order logic

anchor.section: Automated theorem proving and formal methods
anchor.snippet: efficient algorithms are known to decide whether an input finite structure satisfies a first-order formula

annotations.note: Complexity results on model checking finite structures against first-order formulas are not formalized in Mathlib.
